us-politics.headline: U.S. Rescues Airman as Iran War Escalates Sharply
us-politics.body:
U.S. forces rescued a downed airman after military jets were shot down over Iran, marking the first American aircraft lost to enemy fire in over 20 years. Trump declared the war "near completion" while simultaneously escalating threats against Tehran, a tension that drew scrutiny on Sunday political shows. The rescue operation was confirmed by Trump directly.

Analysts across the spectrum are watching how the administration balances military momentum with diplomatic risk. Fox News framed the moment through the lens of wartime presidential burden. Critics question what "near completion" means in practice, given active combat losses. The contradictions are hard to ignore.

sources.title: Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran
Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump College Admissions Order
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's attempt to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions, a condition tied to federal funding. The ruling is a setback for the administration's push to enforce its post-affirmative-action agenda through financial leverage.

Supporters of the order argue institutions have resisted compliance with existing law since the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling against race-conscious admissions. Opponents say the certification requirement was vague and coercive. The legal fight is likely to continue.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions

us-politics.headline: DHS and Van Hollen Clash Over ICE Crash Claim
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security pushed back against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen's account of a crash involving an individual fleeing ICE agents, saying the facts contradict his framing. DHS asserted the driver was an illegal alien who caused the crash while evading law enforcement. Van Hollen had used the incident to criticize ICE tactics.

The dispute reflects a broader pattern of competing narratives around immigration enforcement. Republicans point to incidents like this as evidence of public safety risks from lax border policy. Democrats argue aggressive ICE operations themselves create dangerous situations. Both sides are using the story to reinforce existing positions.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin Supreme Court Race Exposes GOP Weakness
us-politics.body:
Wisconsin Republicans are struggling in a high-profile state Supreme Court race, a result that signals ongoing difficulties translating national momentum into state-level judicial wins. The race has drawn national attention as a test of each party's organizational strength heading into future election cycles.

A vehicle also drove into a crowd at a Louisiana parade, injuring several people. Authorities are investigating. Separately, FBI Director Kash Patel issued a blunt public warning to anyone who attacks law enforcement, saying the bureau would "put you down" - language that drew both support from law-and-order advocates and concern from civil liberties critics.

sources.title: FBI's Patel delivers blunt warning to law enforcement attackers: 'We're going to put you down'
Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles
Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid price crackdown
au-politics.body:
Prime Minister Albanese has secured assurances from Japan and other Asian nations that fuel supplies to Australia will continue normally, as the government works to contain a domestic shortage. The diplomatic push comes as fuel costs bite hard across the country, with farmers reporting they cannot afford to put food on the table and thousands still attending the National Folk Festival despite travel cost concerns.

Authorities are cracking down on price gouging. Nearly 100 NSW service stations have been fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices, and the government has boosted funding for the FuelCheck reporting system, urging motorists to report suspicious hikes. Western Australia is expanding its FuelWatch scheme to cover all regional stations from May.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands
Motorists urged to dob in service stations over fuel price hikes
Thousands flock to National Folk Festival despite fuel cost concerns

au-politics.headline: Murder arrest at Melbourne airport; police shooting in Tasmania
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport on Easter Sunday while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. Police have not yet detailed the circumstances of the alleged killing or the victim's identity.

Separately, Tasmania Police shot a man in the Hobart suburb of Goodwood, leaving him in serious condition. Police have not released details on what prompted the shooting. Both incidents are under investigation.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police

au-politics.headline: Sisters of slain officer to sue Victoria Police over Dezi Freeman
au-politics.body:
The sisters of Senior Constable Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, have announced plans to sue Victoria Police. They describe the process as painful, and the legal action signals deep frustration with how the force handled the circumstances surrounding their brother's death.

Freeman shot Thompson in 2022. The civil action raises questions about police duty of care and what warnings, if any, existed about Freeman before the killing. Victoria Police has not yet publicly responded to the lawsuit announcement.

sources.title: 'Painful': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: 7-year-old girl dies in Adelaide ATV rollover on Easter Sunday
au-politics.body:
A seven-year-old girl died after an all-terrain vehicle rolled in Adelaide's north-east on Easter Sunday. Emergency services attended the scene but were unable to save her. Police are investigating the circumstances of the crash.

The death adds to a grim Easter road toll. Authorities had urged caution over the long weekend, particularly as the North West Coastal Highway reopened after Cyclone Narelle damage, drawing large numbers of travellers back onto regional roads.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle

world.headline: US Rescues Second Pilot as Iran War Escalates
world.body:
A second downed US airman has been rescued from Iran, with Trump confirming the operation and calling it 'miraculous.' The rescue follows ongoing US-Israeli strikes on Iranian territory, including one that left a Tehran psychiatric hospital unusable. Iran has rejected Trump's ultimatums and fired on a Kuwait oil complex, signalling no intention to back down.

The conflict is deepening on multiple fronts. Planet Labs, a US satellite firm, has blacked out imagery of the war zone, restricting independent monitoring of strikes. The US has also arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, a move likely to inflame tensions further. Polling suggests the war remains broadly unpopular with the American public.

sources.title: What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike

world.headline: Iran War Ripples Across Region and Beyond
world.body:
Iran is selectively easing Strait of Hormuz restrictions, allowing Iraqi ships to transit as regional shipping pressure mounts. Pakistan faces growing pressure to navigate the fallout from a conflict on its doorstep, with analysts warning of economic and security consequences. Indonesia received the bodies of UN peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, a reminder that the region's instability is claiming lives well beyond the main front lines.

In Israel, police arrested antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv, reflecting domestic fractures over the country's role in the conflict. The war is reshaping alliances, supply chains, and civil liberties across the Middle East and beyond.

sources.title: Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs for Young Men
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended stays abroad, a striking shift for a country that has long kept conscription off the table. The proposal reflects growing anxiety across Europe about defence readiness as the continent reassesses its security posture.

Separately, rumours of a secessionist 'People's Republic' movement in the Estonian city of Narva have circulated online, though reporting suggests the claims are largely unfounded. Still, the story illustrates how information warfare targeting Baltic states continues to find traction.

sources.title: 'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?
German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad

world.headline: Car Hits Louisiana Parade; Baby Shot Dead in New York
world.body:
A car ploughed into a Lao New Year parade in Louisiana, injuring around 15 people. Authorities are investigating the circumstances, and the incident echoes a series of vehicle attacks on public gatherings seen in recent years across the US.

In New York, a man has been charged with the fatal shooting of a baby in a pram, a killing that drew widespread outrage. The two incidents, unconnected, add to a pattern of public safety crises drawing renewed scrutiny of gun violence and crowd security in American cities.

sources.title: Man charged over fatal shooting of baby in pram in New York
Car slams into Louisiana Lao New Year parade, injuring about 15 people

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Weaponized, Anthropic Adds Paid Tier
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing a leaked version of Anthropic's Claude Code tool bundled with malware, targeting developers who download unofficial copies. The timing is awkward: Anthropic simultaneously announced that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to access OpenClaw support, adding another cost layer to an already premium product.

The dual news puts Anthropic in a defensive position. A leaked tool circulating with malicious payloads erodes trust, while a new paywall risks alienating the developer community the company is actively courting. Security researchers warn that the malware-laced packages are convincingly packaged, making them easy to mistake for legitimate releases.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Folk Musicians
ai.body:
A folk musician found themselves caught between two modern threats: AI-generated fakes mimicking their voice and style, and a copyright troll exploiting the resulting confusion. The case illustrates how AI content generation creates new attack surfaces for bad actors, who can flood platforms with synthetic material and then leverage copyright claims against the original artist.

Meanwhile, the broader question of proving human authorship is sharpening. The Verge reports growing demand for credible "human-made" labels on creative work, as audiences and platforms struggle to distinguish genuine output from AI generation. Neither problem has a clean solution yet.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has flagged a serious regression in Linux 7.0 that roughly halves PostgreSQL performance under certain workloads. The issue appears tied to kernel-level changes that interact poorly with how PostgreSQL manages I/O, and developers warn a fix will not be straightforward.

For cloud infrastructure teams, the timing is painful. Linux 7.0 carries other improvements that make upgrading attractive, but a 50% database performance drop is a hard blocker for anyone running PostgreSQL at scale. The report has surfaced quickly in the kernel community, though no patch timeline has been confirmed.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair Faces Industry Pushback, eIDAS Locks Into Big Tech
ai.body:
Tech companies are lobbying to weaken Colorado's right-to-repair law, one of the strongest in the US, pushing for amendments that would limit which devices and components fall under its protections. The effort follows a familiar playbook used against similar legislation in other states.

Separately, Germany's implementation of the EU's eIDAS digital identity standard has drawn criticism for requiring users to hold an Apple or Google account to function. Critics argue the design hands two American corporations a gatekeeping role over European digital identity infrastructure, undermining the regulation's stated goal of open, sovereign digital ID.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

btc.headline: Drift Protocol Hack Was Months in the Making
btc.body:
The $280 million Drift Protocol exploit was not opportunistic. Preliminary findings show attackers spent months preparing the attack, mapping vulnerabilities and staging their approach before striking. The level of coordination points to a sophisticated, well-resourced threat actor.

The incident adds to a growing list of large-scale DeFi breaches that expose how patient and methodical bad actors have become. Security researchers warn that protocols need to treat threat modeling as an ongoing process, not a one-time audit.

sources.title: Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Faces Quantum Threat as $1.3 Trillion Sits Exposed
btc.body:
Bitcoin's security model, built on cryptographic assumptions from the early 2000s, is drawing urgent scrutiny as quantum computing advances. Several initiatives are now racing to develop quantum-resistant standards before the technology matures enough to crack existing encryption. The stakes are the entire $1.3 trillion market cap sitting on the network.

No quantum computer can break Bitcoin today. But the window to upgrade may be shorter than developers once assumed, and retrofitting a decentralized network with new cryptographic primitives is a slow, politically complex process. The time to start is now.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Bitcoin Bearish Sentiment Hits Five-Week High
btc.body:
Negative chatter around Bitcoin on social media has climbed to its highest level in five weeks, according to Santiment data. Price stagnation is driving the mood shift, with traders growing impatient after an extended period of sideways action.

Historically, peaks in bearish sentiment have coincided with local price bottoms rather than sustained downturns. Santiment analysts suggest the current negativity may be a contrarian signal worth watching, though it offers no guarantee of a near-term reversal.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment

btc.headline: Iran's Telegram Ban Backfired, Says Durov
btc.body:
Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iran's decision to ban the platform produced the opposite of its intended effect, driving more users toward the app and raising its profile inside the country. Durov framed the ban as a case study in how government censorship tends to amplify the reach of the platforms it targets.

The comments carry weight for the crypto community, which relies heavily on Telegram for coordination, project announcements, and community building. Any sustained crackdown on the platform in key markets would create real friction for the industry.

sources.title: Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iranian government's ban backfired

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-roasted leeks lacquered in white miso and honey, scattered with toasted walnuts.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 12 minutes before adding the filling — leeks release moisture and a soggy base will ruin the whole thing.

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks at progressively faster paces — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and back to moderate — training your body to recover while still moving.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, and now being explored in human sensory prosthetics.
learn.why: It upends the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and opens a rabbit hole into how biological systems exploit randomness rather than fight it — with real implications for neuroscience, signal processing, and even decision-making under uncertainty.
